30c3838b-2108-4822-a114-96114899426c.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before any event, the truss is the skeleton that holds up lighting, sound, video and set design. Understanding its anatomy is the first step to building structures that don't fall.

The truss is a modular lattice structure, usually in aluminum alloy, made of chords (main tubes) and welded diagonals/verticals, designed to span gaps with little self-weight. In this lesson you will learn the terminology, the types by section and application, and why the material and geometry define load capacity.

  • Why truss and not a solid tube: A truss distributes stresses through the chords (compression/tension) and the diagonals (shear), achieving high stiffness with low weight. This makes it possible to span large gaps while carrying load (moving heads, line array, screens) without the prohibitive weight of a solid beam. The triangulated geometry is what provides the stiffness.
  • Material: aluminum alloy: Most professional trusses use 6000-series aluminum alloys (e.g. 6082-T6 or 6061-T6) in the tubes, welded with 5000-series filler rod (e.g. 5183). Aluminum is light, corrosion-resistant and has a good strength-to-weight ratio. WARNING: welding reduces local strength (the heat-affected zone), which is why the manufacturer calculates the allowable loads already accounting for this effect — never extrapolate.
  • Types by section: Square/box (4 chords, e.g. Q30S, Q40S from brands such as Prolyte/Global Truss) is the most used because it carries load on any axis. Triangular (3 chords) is lighter, good for smaller spans and decorative truss. Ladder (2 chords, flat) is used to fix lighting bars and backdrops. Circular/arc and frames for portals and set design. Deck/stage is a specific floor structure, with its own standard.
  • Size nomenclature (Q30/Q40): Commercial codes indicate family and section: for example, Prolyte H30V/H40V and Global Truss F33/F34 refer to trusses of ~290–400 mm per side with chord tubes of ~48–50 mm. Always confirm the section and series in the manufacturer's catalog — similar codes are NOT interchangeable and each has its own load chart.
  • Geometry matters: A truss's capacity increases with the height of the section (a longer lever arm between chords = greater resisting moment). That is why a Q40 (40 cm) holds more than a Q30 (30 cm) over the same span. Never compare by appearance alone: the load chart is the reference.

Equipment / details

  • Box truss Q30/Q40 (alloy 6082-T6 / 6061-T6): Main structure for grids, spans and load support on any axis
  • Triangular truss: Smaller spans and lighter decorative structures
  • Ladder truss: Fixing lighting bars, backdrops and curtains
  • Manufacturer's catalog/load chart (Prolyte, Global Truss, Eurotruss, Milos, Tomcat): Official document with allowable loads, deflection and support conditions
  • NBR 8800 / Eurocode 9 (EN 1999) — aluminum calculation reference: Normative basis for structural verification by a qualified professional

Step by step

  1. Identify the truss: Read the label/engraving: brand, series, section. Without reliable identification, do not use it for structural load.
  2. Confirm the material and condition: Check that it is structural aluminum alloy, with no cracks in the weld, no dents in the chords and no white powdery corrosion.
  3. Locate the correct load chart: Download the exact table for the model from the manufacturer's website. Do not use a table from a different brand/series.
  4. Define the section by application: For larger spans and heavy loads, choose a larger section (Q40 before Q30); always confirm in the load chart.
  5. Record the inventory: Note the model, length and condition of each bar for traceability and future inspection.

In practice: At a mid-sized show, the operator arrives at the warehouse, sorts out 2 m and 3 m Q30 bars, checks the Prolyte H30V labels and downloads the corresponding load chart on their phone. They notice that one bar has a chord dented in transport — it is set aside and tagged as 'out of service' until inspection. This simple habit prevents using a piece with compromised capacity.

Summary

  • The truss is an aluminum lattice structure that spans gaps with little weight thanks to its triangulated geometry.
  • Types: box/square (most versatile), triangular, ladder, circular and deck — each with its own application.
  • A larger section (Q40 > Q30) carries more load, but the reference is always the manufacturer's official load chart.

a72983a7-48fa-43d5-9b4c-569e59d4e035.titulo: Load charts, UDL, point load and deflection
a72983a7-48fa-43d5-9b4c-569e59d4e035.conteudo_rico:
To begin: A truss never 'holds just any weight'. It holds a specific weight, over a specific span, distributed in a specific way. Ignoring this is the No. 1 cause of collapses.

Here you learn to read and respect the load chart: the difference between distributed load (UDL) and point load, how the free span reduces capacity, and the concept of deflection — how much the truss 'sags' under load. Structural calculation of scenarios outside the catalog is the job of a qualified engineer.

  • What the load chart is: It is the manufacturer's table that cross-references free span (distance between supports) with maximum allowable load, usually already with a safety factor applied. The larger the span, the lower the permitted load. The chart usually also gives the resulting deflection and the support configuration considered (simply supported, with or without a central bridge).
  • UDL — uniformly distributed load: It is load spread over the entire length (e.g. kg/m or total kg distributed). Many charts express capacity as UDL. A row of evenly spaced fixtures approximates a UDL. It is the most favorable way to load the truss.
  • Point load: It is load concentrated at a single point. A single hoist, a line array cluster or a screen hung from one node is a point load. The same truss holds LESS point load at the center than total UDL, because it concentrates the bending moment. Serious charts have separate columns: 1 central point, 2 points at 1/3, etc.
  • Free span and number of supports: Doubling the span drastically reduces capacity (the moment grows with the square of the span). Adding a support/rigging point in the middle greatly increases capacity and reduces deflection. That is why big shows use several hoists along the truss, not just at the ends.
  • Deflection: It is how much the truss drops at the center under load. Even within capacity, a large deflection looks bad aesthetically and may indicate that you are at the limit. Manufacturers usually limit deflection (e.g. L/100 of the span as a catalog reference) — follow the chart value. Excessive deflection is a warning to reduce load or shorten the span.
  • Safety factor and when to call an engineer: Load charts already include a safety factor (often on the order of 2:1 over allowable loads, depending on the manufacturer). For configurations outside the catalog — combined spans, mixed loads, wind on outdoor structures, cantilevered truss — verification must be done by an engineer with ART/RRT. Do not improvise calculations.

Equipment / details

  • Model load chart (Prolyte/Global Truss/Eurotruss/Milos/Tomcat): Defines allowable load by span, load type and deflection
  • Tape measure/laser measurer: Measure the actual free span between supports
  • Scale/datasheet of the hung equipment: Know the real weight of each fixture, hoist and cluster
  • Rigging plot spreadsheet / software (e.g. manufacturer's calculation): Add up loads, position points and verify limits
  • Qualified engineer (ART/RRT) — NBR / Eurocode 9: Signed calculation for configurations outside the catalog (SAFETY)

Step by step

  1. Measure the actual free span: Use the effective distance between supports, not the estimate. The chart depends on it.
  2. List and add up the real loads: Take the datasheet weight of each item (fixture, hoist, cable, cluster) and add them, including the truss's own weight.
  3. Classify the load: Distinguish what is distributed (UDL) from what is point load (cluster, screen) and use the correct chart column.
  4. Compare with the chart: Check that the total load and the per-point load are below the allowable for that span. Consider the indicated deflection.
  5. Reduce span or add support if necessary: If you exceed the limit, add an intermediate rigging point or shorten the span; reassess.
  6. Bring in an engineer for what falls outside the catalog: Cantilevers, outdoor wind, combined loads and atypical structures require signed calculation.

In practice: A designer wants to hang a 180 kg line array cluster at the center of a Q40 with a 10 m span. In the load chart, the allowable central point load for 10 m is below that. Solution adopted: add a central hoist, turning a 10 m span into two 5 m spans. The point capacity rises, deflection drops by half and the cluster is safe — all read from the table, no guessing.

Summary

  • The load chart cross-references span x load x type (UDL or point) and already includes a safety factor — it is the truss's law.
  • A central point load is more severe than UDL; more span reduces capacity by the square, more supports increase it.
  • Deflection is the truss's 'sag'; respect the chart limit and call an engineer for cases outside the catalog.

070c8c45-70ed-4c2e-a4b2-88ef8ba389e0.titulo: Truss connections: block, spigot/cone, connector and assembly
070c8c45-70ed-4c2e-a4b2-88ef8ba389e0.conteudo_rico:
To begin: A perfect truss connected the wrong way becomes a hazard. The joints are where load passes from one bar to another — and where most mistakes happen.

This lesson covers how the bars join: corner block, the spigot/cone system with pins and R-clips (Prolyte CCS6) or bolt/flange connection (Global Truss), plus good practices for ground assembly before hoisting. A wrong connection voids the load chart.

  • Block and corner block: These are the corner blocks that allow a change of direction (forming frames, portals, grids). The block must be from the same system/series as the truss and have the same load class. Mixing in a lower-capacity block creates the structure's weak link.
  • Spigot/cone, pin and R-clip: In systems like Prolyte CCS6/CCS7, the joint is made by a cone (spigot) inserted between the bars, locked by two pins and their R-clips (safety clips). ALL pins and R-clips must be fitted; a missing R-clip is a serious failure that can release the joint under vibration.
  • Bolt/flange connection: Systems such as the Global Truss F-series join with bolts (M-bolts) with nut and washer, in flange holes. Tighten as per the manufacturer, without improvising a bolt of a different gauge/class. An ordinary hardware bolt does not have the required strength class.
  • Compatibility between brands: Do not assume that trusses from different brands fit together or have the same capacity just because the cone 'goes in'. Use official adapters where they exist and respect the lowest load chart involved. Connecting a Q30 with a Q40 completely changes the behavior.
  • Ground assembly: Assemble as much as possible on the ground, on trestles, checking alignment and every connection, before hoisting. It is safer and faster than connecting up high. Mark the orientation (the load chart's preferred load) to raise it in the correct direction.

Equipment / details

  • Cones/spigots, pins and R-clips (e.g. Prolyte CCS6): Structural joint locked between bars of the same system
  • Bolts/nuts of the specified class (e.g. Global Truss F34): Flange connection per the manufacturer's torque
  • Block/corner block of the same series and class: Change of direction while keeping the structure's capacity
  • Assembly trestles: Assemble and check the truss on the ground safely
  • Rubber mallet and correct wrenches: Fit cones and tighten connections without damaging the aluminum

Step by step

  1. Sort out components from the same system: Confirm that bars, blocks, cones and pins are of the same series and load class.
  2. Assemble on trestles: Rest the bars aligned and join them on the ground, avoiding unnecessary work at height (NR-35).
  3. Insert cones and lock pins: Fit the cone, install BOTH pins and secure ALL R-clips/clips — check one by one.
  4. Tighten bolts to torque: On flange systems, use the correct bolt and the torque indicated by the manufacturer.
  5. Check alignment and orientation: Verify there is no bent bar and that the highest-load face is in the direction foreseen by the load chart.
  6. Double inspection before hoisting: A second professional reviews all joints; only then does the structure go up.

In practice: During the assembly of a portal, the assistant forgets an R-clip on a joint up high. In the double check (final step), the rigging lead goes over each cone and detects the missing clip before hoisting. The joint is completed on the ground. Without this double-checking routine, the failure would only appear under vibration, at the worst moment.

Summary

  • The joints transfer the load; block, cone and pins must be from the same system and class.
  • A missing R-clip or the wrong bolt voids the load chart and can release the joint under vibration.
  • Assemble and check on the ground, mark the orientation and do a double check before hoisting.

928de739-5cda-49c7-9da6-e9ddc9a5e4c8.titulo: Rigging and hoisting: hoists, points, slings and redundancy
928de739-5cda-49c7-9da6-e9ddc9a5e4c8.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Flying a structure over the audience's heads is the most critical operation of the event. Everything here is a matter of life — there is no room for improvisation.

Rigging is the art and engineering of suspending loads safely. You will see chain hoists (electric hoists) like the CM Lodestar and ChainMaster, rigging points and their capacity, steel wire rope slings and round slings, shackles, the difference between ground support and a flown structure, safety factor and the mandatory nature of redundancy. NR-35 (work at height) and NR-18 are the legal backdrop.

  • Electric hoist / motor (chain hoist): Equipment that hoists the truss by a link chain. Reference models: CM Lodestar and ChainMaster. Typical capacities at events: 1/2 t and 1 t. They must be certified, with maintenance up to date, and be operated by a suitable controller. Entertainment rigging requires a hoist of good origin, not an improvised mechanic's-shop hoist.
  • Direct vs. redundant hoisting (D8/D8+/BGV-C1/IGVW SQ P2): Lifting loads OVER PEOPLE has stricter requirements than lifting industrial load. Reference European standards (BGV-C1, now IGVW SQ P2/SQ P1, and the D8/D8+ classification of hoists) define conditions for lifting over an audience — including redundancy. Know and follow the standard required in your area and contract.
  • Rigging points and capacity: Each point (venue hook, beam, ground support) has a capacity that must be known and documented. Hanging from a point without proven capacity is like hanging from the unknown. In building structures, the point's capacity must be confirmed by the party responsible for the building/an engineer.
  • Slings: steel wire rope and round sling: Slings connect the truss to the hoist or to the point. They can be steel wire rope or textile round sling (polyester). Each sling has a labeled WLL (Working Load Limit). The sling angle reduces capacity: the wider the angle, the greater the force in each leg. Protect textile round slings from sharp edges with protectors.
  • Shackles: The shackle connects sling, point and hoist. Use rigging shackles marked with WLL (e.g. Crosby type or certified equivalent), never an unmarked hardware shackle. The threaded pin must be fully tightened. The shackle's capacity must be equal to or greater than that of the assembly.
  • Safety factor and WLL: WLL is the safe working load, already divided from the breaking load by a factor (often 5:1 for lifting accessories, per the component's standard). Never operate above the WLL. Add up the load's real weight and confirm that each link in the chain (hoist, sling, shackle, point) has sufficient WLL.
  • Redundancy / safety cable: For loads over people, redundancy is required: a second load path (safety/steel) that holds the equipment if the primary one fails. Fixtures and hung accessories carry a safety bond with adequate WLL. Redundancy is NOT optional in rigging over an audience.
  • NR-35 and NR-18: NR-35 governs work at height: risk analysis, work permit, harness/lanyard with double hook anchored to a strong point, training and supervision. NR-18 covers safety in the construction/assembly of structures. Every crew member who climbs on the truss needs valid training and adequate PPE.

Equipment / details

  • Electric hoist / chain hoist (CM Lodestar, ChainMaster) 1/2 t–1 t certified: Controlled hoisting of the truss
  • Motor controller: Operate motors individually or in a group safely
  • Steel wire rope slings and textile round slings with labeled WLL: Connection between truss, point and hoist
  • Certified rigging shackles with marked WLL (e.g. Crosby): Joining slings, points and hoists
  • Safety cable / safety bond with WLL: Mandatory redundancy on equipment over people (SAFETY)
  • Height PPE: full-body harness, double lanyard, helmet (NR-35): Protection of the crew in work at height
  • Dynamometer / load cell: Measure the real load on each hoist/point

Step by step

  1. Do the risk analysis and the work permit: Before going up, assess risks, define anchor points and issue a work permit (NR-35).
  2. Confirm the capacity of the points: Verify and document the WLL of each rigging point with the party responsible for the structure/an engineer.
  3. Size the hoisting assembly: Add up the real load and choose hoist, slings and shackles with sufficient WLL, considering the sling angle.
  4. Inspect each component: Check the chain, the hook with latch, the slings (no broken wires), the marked shackles and the tightened pin. Discard anything damaged.
  5. Install the redundancy: Add a safety cable on equipment and, over an audience, ensure a second load path per the required standard.
  6. Hoist in a controlled, level way: Raise slowly, watching all motors and the structure; stop at any sign of uneven load or abnormal noise.
  7. Lock and cordon off the area: With the load at the point, block access below while people are working above.

In practice: To fly a truss with a line array, the crew issues the work permit, confirms with the venue's engineer that each ceiling hook holds 1 t, and measures 720 kg on each motor with a load cell. Steel slings with a 2 t WLL and 2 t Crosby shackles complete the assembly with margin. Each array box has its safety. When one motor rises faster, the operator stops everything and re-levels — uneven load is never ignored.

Summary

  • Lifting over an audience requires a certified hoist, WLL respected in every link and mandatory redundancy.
  • Slings and shackles have a labeled WLL; a wide sling angle increases the force in each leg.
  • NR-35 and NR-18 govern the operation: risk analysis, PPE, strong anchorage and a trained crew are non-negotiable.

6bbea573-a7be-4071-af7b-c22726ad909d.titulo: Ground support, flying and structural stability
6bbea573-a7be-4071-af7b-c22726ad909d.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Not every structure is hung. Often you raise it from the floor — and then the challenge is to keep it from toppling.

Ground support means towers/columns that hold the truss up from the ground, with sleeve blocks and motors that raise the grid up the tower itself. This lesson covers bases, ballast, guy wires, wind and the practical difference between flying (flown) and supporting (ground). Outdoor structures have the added challenge of wind — always an engineer's design.

  • Ground support: how it works: Vertical truss towers (sleeve block + tower) receive the horizontal grid and raise it with internal motors. It is used when there is no ceiling point, in arenas, gyms and open-air shows. The towers transfer all the load to the floor, which needs to withstand the reaction.
  • Base, footing and load distribution: Each tower rests on a base (base plate) that distributes the load to the ground. On weak or uneven floors use distribution plates. The reaction at the foot includes the structure's weight plus any wind effect — check the floor/ground capacity.
  • Ballast and stability: To prevent toppling, mainly with wind, ballast (counterweight) and/or guy wires are used. The amount of ballast and the guying scheme are defined by an engineer's calculation, considering the sail area (screen, banner, tarpaulin) and the design wind speed. A banner GREATLY increases wind force.
  • Guy wires: Tensioned cables that anchor the structure to the ground, resisting lateral forces (wind). Angle, anchor point and tension are part of the design. Poorly anchored guy wires are as dangerous as absent ones.
  • Flying (flown) vs. supporting (ground): Flying hangs from the existing ceiling/point — it depends on that point's capacity. Supporting (ground support) is independent of the ceiling, but requires towers, base and stability against toppling. The choice depends on the venue, the load and the capacity of the available points.
  • Wind and the outdoor environment: Outdoors, wind is the dominant and variable load. Outdoor structures require a design with a wind management plan: limit speeds to operate, stop the show and evacuate/lower the structure. Monitor with an anemometer. There is no safe outdoor stage without a wind plan signed by an engineer.

Equipment / details

  • Ground support towers (sleeve block + tower): Raise the grid from the ground without depending on the ceiling
  • Base plates and load-distribution plates: Distribute the tower's reaction on weak or uneven floors
  • Ballast (counterweights): Stability against toppling, sized by an engineer (SAFETY)
  • Guy wires and anchors: Resist lateral and wind forces
  • Anemometer: Measure wind speed and trigger the management plan
  • Engineer's design/ART for the outdoor structure: Define ballast, guy wires and wind limits (SAFETY)

Step by step

  1. Assess the floor/ground: Confirm that the floor withstands the towers' reaction; use distribution plates where needed.
  2. Assemble plumb towers: Raise the towers level and plumb, following the ground support system's manual.
  3. Install ballast and guy wires per the design: Apply the counterweight and the guying exactly as the engineer specified for the sail area and wind.
  4. Raise the grid in a synchronized way: Raise the grid with the towers' motors levelly, watching stability.
  5. Check plumb and tensions: Re-check the towers' plumb and the guy wires' tension after the grid is in place.
  6. Activate the wind plan: Position the anemometer and define limits for operation, stopping and lowering the structure.

In practice: At an open-air stage with a 5 x 3 m screen, the sail area increases the wind force on the towers. The engineer's design calls for 600 kg of ballast per tower and guy wires at 45°. On the day, the anemometer reads increasing gusts; on reaching the plan's limit, the producer stops the show and the crew lowers the grid. The decision saves the structure — wind does not negotiate.

Summary

  • Ground support raises the grid from the floor via towers; all the load goes to the floor, which must withstand it.
  • Ballast and guy wires ensure stability against toppling and must be sized by an engineer.
  • On outdoor structures, wind rules: anemometer, operating limits and a lowering plan are mandatory.

1d327eed-70d2-4786-b4bd-540e51e619d9.titulo: Power: distribution, panels, phases and cable sizing
1d327eed-70d2-4786-b4bd-540e51e619d9.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Without well-done power, no show happens — and poorly-done power kills. Grounding, sizing and protection are the heart of electrical safety.

This lesson covers the event's power chain: incoming supply, distribution panel, breakers and RCD, power connectors (CEE/IEC 60309, SocaPex, PowerCON, NBR 14136), three-phase vs. single-phase, phase balancing, load and cable sizing (gauge x current x voltage drop), grounding, generator and UPS. NR-10 governs all of this. Design and execution in large installations require a qualified professional.

  • Distribution chain: Power comes from the incoming supply (utility or generator), passes through a distribution panel (main panel/power panel) with protections, and branches out to areas (stage, PA, lighting, video). Each branch has a breaker suited to its load. The panel must be intended for temporary use, enclosed and protected against contact and weather.
  • Breakers and RCD: Breakers protect against overload and short circuit. The RCD (residual current device) protects people against shock from leakage current, tripping quickly (typically 30 mA for the protection of people). Circuits accessible to the public and wet areas must have an RCD. A breaker does NOT replace an RCD — they protect against different things.
  • Three-phase vs. single-phase and balancing: A three-phase grid delivers three phases (e.g. ~220/380 V depending on the region) plus neutral and ground; single-phase uses one phase. Distribute the loads evenly across the three phases (balancing) so as not to overload one phase and to avoid excessive current in the neutral. Lighting on one phase, sound on another, video on another is a start — then adjust by the real consumption.
  • Power connectors: CEE/IEC 60309 (blue single-phase and red three-phase plugs, with pins and IP ratings) is the robust standard for power. SocaPex (multipin, 19-pin type = 6 circuits) concentrates several lighting circuits in one cable. PowerCON (Neutrik) is a locking connector for audio/video equipment. NBR 14136 is the Brazilian general-purpose plug/socket standard. Use the right connector for each application — never splice bare wire.
  • Load sizing: Add up the power (W) of everything on the circuit and divide by the voltage to get the current (A). The breaker and cable must handle that current with margin. Do not fill a circuit to the breaker's limit — leave headroom for peaks (e.g. motor startup, discharge lamps).
  • Cable gauge x current x distance (voltage drop): The cable cross-section (mm² in ABNT; AWG in the American standard) must handle the current without overheating AND without excessive voltage drop over long distances. The greater the current and distance, the larger the required gauge. High voltage drop makes equipment fail and cables heat up. For long distances, increase the gauge — follow a technical table and standard (NBR 5410 for low voltage).
  • Grounding and equipotential bonding: The ground (PE) carries fault currents to earth and references the system, allowing the RCD/breaker to act and preventing enclosures from becoming energized. Equipotential bonding interconnects the metallic masses (trusses, stage, racks) to ground so there is no dangerous potential difference between them. Deficient grounding is a common cause of shock and of noise in audio.
  • Generator and UPS: A generator supplies power where there is no grid or as a backup; it must be sized with margin, grounded and with safe transfer. A UPS keeps critical loads (consoles, network, computers) running during drops/transitions. For shows, feeding the console rack from a UPS avoids a reboot in the middle of the show.
  • NR-10: NR-10 governs safety in electrical installations and services: authorized and trained professionals, design, records, control measures, PPE and procedures. Large event installations must have a technical officer. Power is not an area for amateurs.

Equipment / details

  • Temporary distribution panel with breakers and RCD: Protect circuits against overload/short and people against shock (SAFETY)
  • CEE/IEC 60309 connectors (blue single-phase, red three-phase): Robust power connection with an IP rating
  • SocaPex cables (multipin): Concentrate several lighting circuits in a single cable
  • PowerCON (Neutrik) and NBR 14136 plugs: Locked feed for audio/video and general-purpose sockets
  • Cables with sized gauge (mm²/AWG) per table and NBR 5410: Carry current without overheating or dropping too much voltage
  • Ground rod/system and equipotential bonding bar: Protective ground and interconnection of the metallic masses (SAFETY)
  • Sized generator and UPS: Supply and continuity for critical loads
  • Clamp meter and multimeter: Measure current per phase and check voltage/ground continuity

Step by step

  1. Work out the total and per-circuit load: Add up the powers, calculate the current and define breakers and gauges with margin (NBR 5410).
  2. Build the panel with protection and RCD: Use an enclosed temporary panel, with the correct breakers and an RCD on the required circuits.
  3. Do the grounding first: Establish the ground and the equipotential bonding of the masses before energizing (SAFETY).
  4. Balance the phases: Distribute the branches across L1/L2/L3 and measure with a clamp meter to balance the currents.
  5. Size cables by distance: For long runs, increase the gauge to limit voltage drop; avoid improvised splices.
  6. Test before releasing: Check ground continuity, RCD tripping (test button) and the absence of voltage on enclosures.
  7. Protect and mark: Cover cables with cable ramps, isolate the panel from the public and identify each circuit.

In practice: At an event with a generator, the electrician adds up 18 kW of lighting, 6 kW of sound and 3 kW of video. He splits the loads across the three phases seeking balance and measures with a clamp meter: 28 A, 31 A and 26 A — acceptable. The ground is driven and tested, all public circuits have a 30 mA RCD, and the 60 m run to the stage uses a larger gauge to hold the voltage drop. Everything is tested with the RCD button before releasing.

Summary

  • From incoming supply to branch: a protected panel, a breaker against overload and an RCD against shock to people.
  • Balance the three phases and size the cable by current AND distance (voltage drop).
  • Grounding and equipotential bonding are vital; NR-10 requires a qualified professional and procedures.

d9856087-bc37-4c4c-a912-79e477c4c619.titulo: Cables and connectors by signal: organization and protection
d9856087-bc37-4c4c-a912-79e477c4c619.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Audio, lighting, video and network only work if the right cable carries the right signal. And a cable on the floor without protection is a trip, an accident and a failure waiting to happen.

Each type of signal has its cable and connector, its length limits and its good practices. You will see XLR/TRS (audio), speakON (loudspeaker), DMX (lighting), HDMI/SDI/fiber (video), etherCON (network) and power, plus cable management (dressing) and protection. The wrong connector degrades the signal and creates risk.

  • Audio: XLR and TRS: Balanced XLR is the standard for microphones and line signals over long distances (it rejects noise). TRS/1/4-inch also carries balanced/instrument audio. Balanced audio runs tens of meters with low noise; unbalanced is short. Never use a speaker cable as a signal cable or vice versa.
  • Loudspeaker: speakON: speakON (Neutrik) is the locking connector for loudspeaker power between amplifier and cabinet, with a gauge suited to the current. It replaces plugs that could expose energized contacts. For power, the conductor gauge matters: a cabinet far from the amp calls for a thicker conductor.
  • Lighting: DMX (5-pin XLR): DMX512 travels in a cable specific for data (impedance ~120 ohm), ideally a 5-pin XLR connector. Do NOT use a microphone cable for DMX on long links — the wrong impedance causes errors and flickering. Use a 120 ohm terminator at the end of the line and respect the device/length limits per universe.
  • Video: HDMI, SDI and fiber: HDMI is short by nature (a few reliable meters on passive copper). SDI (coaxial BNC) runs much farther and is the professional standard. For long distances use optical fiber or extenders. Choose by distance and resolution, not by what is at hand.
  • Network: etherCON: etherCON (Neutrik) is the reinforced, locking RJ45 for audio/lighting/video networks (Dante, sACN, Art-Net) in a stage environment. Copper Ethernet cable has a practical limit of ~100 m per run; beyond that, use fiber. The network is today the backbone of many systems.
  • Management (dressing) and protection: Organize cables with slack, separating power from signal to reduce interference, and identify the ends. Protect crossings with cable ramps or trays, especially where the public circulates. A loose cable on the floor is a trip risk and a risk of damage to the cable itself. Make service loops and avoid strain on the connectors.

Equipment / details

  • XLR and TRS cables (balanced audio): Microphones and line signals with low noise over distance
  • speakON cables (Neutrik) with adequate gauge: Amplifier power to the speaker cabinet in a locked way
  • DMX cable (~120 ohm) with 5-pin XLR and terminator: Lighting control (DMX512) without data errors
  • SDI/BNC, HDMI and optical fiber cables: Video according to distance and resolution
  • etherCON / Cat5e-Cat6 cables: Stage networks (Dante, sACN, Art-Net) locked and protected
  • Cable ramps, trays and identification tapes: Protection against tripping/damage and organization (SAFETY)

Step by step

  1. Choose the cable by the signal: Use the specific cable for each signal (DMX for lighting, SDI/fiber for long video, balanced for audio).
  2. Respect the distance limits: Know the practical reach: HDMI short, SDI/fiber long, Ethernet ~100 m per run.
  3. Separate power from signal: Keep power cables away from signal cables to reduce interference/noise.
  4. Terminate and identify: On DMX, use a 120 ohm terminator; label all ends for quick diagnosis.
  5. Protect crossings: Cover crossings with cable ramps or trays, especially where the public circulates (NR-35/NR-18 in the surroundings).
  6. Leave a service loop: Avoid strain on the connector with some slack near the equipment.

In practice: At a festival, the lighting tech uses a 120 ohm DMX cable with a 5-pin XLR and a terminator on the last moving head — before, with a microphone cable, the lights flickered. The 40 m video run to the screen is done in SDI, not HDMI. Where the cables cross the public corridor, yellow cable ramps go in. Result: a clean signal and nobody trips.

Summary

  • Each signal requires its cable: balanced audio/XLR, speakON, 120 ohm DMX, SDI/fiber, etherCON.
  • Respect the distance limits and separate power from signal to preserve quality.
  • Protect crossings with cable ramps and identify everything — organization is safety and diagnosis.

bdc695ca-2190-4973-809e-4944e5778e51.titulo: Standards and norms: DMX/RDM/Art-Net/sACN, IP/IK and plugs
bdc695ca-2190-4973-809e-4944e5778e51.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Behind every cable and piece of equipment there is a standard that ensures everything talks to each other, resists water and fits together safely. Knowing the standards means speaking the industry's technical language.

Here we consolidate the standards: lighting control protocols (DMX512 ANSI E1.11, RDM E1.20, Art-Net, sACN E1.31), the IP protection rating (IPxx) and IK against impacts, and the plug standards (IEC 60309/CEE, NBR 14136, NEMA), with the role of ABNT standards and the NRs. You learn what each standard GUARANTEES — and why it matters for safety and reliability.

  • DMX512 (ANSI E1.11): The standard lighting control protocol: one universe = 512 channels, on a serial link (cable ~120 ohm, 5-pin XLR), in a daisy chain with termination. It ensures that consoles and fixtures from different brands understand each other. It is unidirectional (controller -> devices).
  • RDM (ANSI E1.20): Remote Device Management runs over the same DMX cable and adds bidirectional communication: discovering devices, addressing remotely, reading status/temperature/errors. It ensures diagnosis and configuration without climbing the truss to adjust each fixture.
  • Art-Net and sACN (E1.31): They carry DMX over an Ethernet/IP network, transporting many universes over one network cable. Art-Net is widely used; sACN (ANSI E1.31) is standardized and efficient (multicast). They allow scaling to hundreds of universes and integrating media servers, consoles and nodes. They require a well-designed network (etherCON/fiber).
  • IP protection rating (IPxx): The IP code defines the enclosure's protection: first digit = solids/dust (0–6), second = water (0–8/9K). E.g.: IP20 (indoor use, no water protection), IP65 (dust-tight and against water jets — for outdoor use). For rain and an outdoor stage, demand the appropriate IP; an IP20 device out in the open is an electrical risk.
  • IK rating (impact): The IK code (e.g. IK08, IK10) indicates the enclosure's resistance to mechanical impacts. Useful for fixtures and panels subject to knocks. It complements IP: a device may be watertight (high IP) but fragile to impact (low IK) — assess both.
  • Plugs: IEC 60309/CEE, NBR 14136, NEMA: IEC 60309 (CEE) is the robust industrial standard (blue single-phase, red three-phase), with an IP rating and locking — ideal for event power. NBR 14136 is the Brazilian general-purpose plug/socket. NEMA is the North American standard (important when using imported equipment). They ensure correct fit, polarity and protection; using an improvised adapter breaks those guarantees.
  • ABNT and the NRs: ABNT publishes the Brazilian technical standards (e.g. NBR 5410 low-voltage installations, NBR 5419 protection against lightning). The NRs (NR-10 electrical, NR-35 height, NR-18 construction/assembly) are workplace regulatory standards, of mandatory compliance. Technical standard + safety norm go together.
  • What each standard guarantees: In short: protocols (DMX/RDM/Art-Net/sACN) guarantee INTEROPERABILITY and control; IP/IK guarantee the equipment's PROTECTION against the environment and impact; plug standards guarantee a safe and correct CONNECTION; ABNT/NRs guarantee legal COMPLIANCE and safety. Ignoring any of them trades reliability for risk.

Equipment / details

  • Console + DMX512 (ANSI E1.11) fixtures with terminator: Interoperable lighting control across brands
  • Equipment with RDM (ANSI E1.20): Remote diagnosis and addressing over the DMX cable
  • Art-Net / sACN (E1.31) nodes and managed switches: Transport of many universes over an IP network
  • Equipment with an adequate IP/IK rating (e.g. IP65 for outdoor): Protection against dust, water and impact (SAFETY)
  • IEC 60309/CEE, NBR 14136 plugs and certified NEMA adapters: Correct and safe power connection by standard
  • ABNT standards (NBR 5410/5419) and NRs (10/35/18): Legal and safety basis for the whole operation (SAFETY)

Step by step

  1. Choose the protocol by scale: Few circuits: DMX/RDM directly; many universes: Art-Net/sACN over a well-designed network.
  2. Terminate and address correctly: Use a DMX terminator and take advantage of RDM to address/diagnose without climbing the truss.
  3. Check the IP/IK for the environment: Outdoor/rain requires a high IP (e.g. IP65); environments with impact risk call for an adequate IK.
  4. Match plugs by standard: Use IEC 60309 for power, NBR 14136 for general use and certified NEMA adapters on imported equipment.
  5. Confirm normative compliance: Check NBR 5410 (installation), NBR 5419 (lightning) and the applicable NRs with the technical officer.
  6. Document the system: Record universes, addresses, network IPs and standards used for maintenance and diagnosis.

In practice: On a tour with 12 lighting universes, the system uses sACN over a network with managed switches and etherCON, converted to DMX at the nodes next to the trusses, with RDM to address floor fixtures. The equipment on the covered stage is IP54; that on the outdoor grid, IP65. The power uses red three-phase CEE plugs. Everything documented: each universe, IP and address — maintenance in minutes, not hours.

Summary

  • DMX512/RDM/Art-Net/sACN guarantee interoperability and control; choose by the system's scale.
  • IP protects against dust/water and IK against impact; combine the two according to the environment.
  • Standardized plugs (IEC 60309/NBR 14136/NEMA) and ABNT/NR standards guarantee safe connection and compliance.

ec96974c-99a0-4c58-a54e-48eb385ad812.titulo: From improvisation to professional: choosing reliable solutions
ec96974c-99a0-4c58-a54e-48eb385ad812.conteudo_rico:
To begin: There is always the cheap, quick way — and the right way. In structure and power, the difference between the two can be the difference between a show and a tragedy.

This final lesson brings everything together in a practical comparison: from basic to professional, in truss, rigging and power, showing where improvisation is acceptable (with judgment) and where it is simply prohibited. The common thread is risk management and technical responsibility.

  • Truss: improvisation vs. reliable: PROFESSIONAL: identified-brand truss, load chart, calculated span and loads, complete joints. DANGEROUS IMPROVISATION: scaffold tube or unidentified truss as a load support over people, span 'by eye', mixing systems. A structure over the public is never improvisation — it is design.
  • Rigging: improvisation vs. reliable: PROFESSIONAL: certified hoist, slings and shackles with WLL, redundancy, NR-35 crew, load cell. DANGEROUS IMPROVISATION: rope/wire, unmarked hardware shackle, lifting over the public without redundancy, no one trained. Lifting over heads is the operation of greatest consequence — zero improvisation.
  • Power: improvisation vs. reliable: PROFESSIONAL: panel with breaker and RCD, tested grounding, balanced phases, sized cable, NR-10. DANGEROUS IMPROVISATION: a 'jury-rig' with bare wire, stacked power strips, no RCD, no ground, an undersized cable heating up. Poorly-done power is fire and shock waiting to happen.
  • Risk management and technical responsibility: Large events require technical officers (electrical/structural engineer with ART), risk analysis, and in many cases a fire-department occupancy certificate (AVCB)/reports and city hall approvals. Knowing the limit of one's own competence and calling the qualified professional is the mark of a mature technician, not weakness.
  • When to say no: There are times when the right answer is to stop: wind above the limit, an unidentified structure for load, a rigging point without proven capacity, an electrical installation without a ground. Saying 'we don't build it this way' protects lives and protects you legally. Safety comes before the show.
  • Safety culture and checklists: The reliable professional standardizes: uses checklists, does double checks, documents, periodically inspects equipment and trains the crew. Safety is not an isolated act, it is a team habit. Every lesson in this module brought a checklist — turn them into routine.

Equipment / details

  • Certified and identified equipment (truss, hoist, slings, panel): Ensure known and traceable capacity (SAFETY)
  • Documentation: load charts, WLL, ART/RRT, risk analysis, reports: Prove sizing and technical responsibility
  • Checklists from the previous lessons: Standardize verification and the double check
  • PPE and instruments (anemometer, load cell, clamp meter): Operate and measure safely
  • Qualified technical officer (engineer): Sign structure and power designs and reports (SAFETY)

Step by step

  1. Classify each item as basic/pro: For truss, rigging and power, assess whether the solution is reliable or dangerous improvisation.
  2. Eliminate the prohibited improvisations: Structure/rigging over the public and power without ground/RCD have no acceptable 'improvised' version.
  3. Ensure technical responsibility: For a relevant scale, secure the ART/RRT, risk analysis and required reports (fire department/city hall).
  4. Apply the checklists: Use each lesson's lists as a routine of verification and double check.
  5. Exercise the right of refusal: Faced with an unsafe condition (wind, unknown point, lack of ground), stop and correct.
  6. Document and improve: Record what was done, inspect equipment and train the crew continuously.

In practice: A client asks to hang a heavy screen from a ceiling point 'that has always held'. The mature technician does not accept 'it has always held': he requests confirmation of capacity from the building's engineer. Without a document, he proposes ground support with calculated towers. It costs a little more, but the structure is safe and backed up. Saying no to improvisation was the professional decision.

Summary

  • From basic to pro: the difference is known capacity, sizing and technical responsibility.
  • Load over people and power without ground/RCD have no acceptable improvised version — they are prohibited.
  • Safety culture is a habit: checklists, double checks, documentation and the right to say no.

fa2e2894-05b8-40f5-b062-632eb59460d2.pergunta: Why does a truss support a lot of load with little self-weight?
fa2e2894-05b8-40f5-b062-632eb59460d2.explicacao: Triangulation distributes compression/tension in the chords and shear in the diagonals, giving high stiffness with low weight.

opcoes.b3a2c871-05e8-4171-bc40-72361765fc94: Because aluminum is heavier than steel
opcoes.8049ee56-051a-432f-8527-24c5fa7a610c: Because the triangulated geometry distributes stresses between chords and diagonals
opcoes.af15d1a7-603d-4cb4-b968-c3bfc17017e3: Because it is made of solid steel

58b38af8-0cf4-4480-be78-84c983ad20bf.pergunta: What does welding cause in the strength of the truss's aluminum?
58b38af8-0cf4-4480-be78-84c983ad20bf.explicacao: Welding creates a heat-affected zone with lower strength; that is why the manufacturer already builds this into the allowable loads — do not extrapolate.

opcoes.860efebe-4b4b-4798-8cc1-bc9ceb20dfe2: It reduces strength in the heat-affected zone, already accounted for in the load chart
opcoes.5d523853-caf8-4112-8942-f464c4cb6a97: It turns the aluminum into steel

728d95ec-176a-4eaa-914b-4cabf59609df.pergunta: Between Q30 and Q40 over the same span, which tends to support more load and why?
728d95ec-176a-4eaa-914b-4cabf59609df.explicacao: A greater section height increases the resisting moment, raising capacity — but always confirm in the load chart.

opcoes.7b908064-1086-4260-835b-7448b753834e: It depends only on the color
opcoes.1fbdc486-b070-44d2-bdb0-16576aede173: Q40, because of the greater section height (longer arm between chords)

ce6a1fad-3f1e-4c06-a751-a8af0a28bae6.pergunta: What is the correct source for a truss's load capacity?
ce6a1fad-3f1e-4c06-a751-a8af0a28bae6.explicacao: Each model/series has its own load chart; tables from different brands or series are not interchangeable.

opcoes.b734aeed-8a23-4645-a2e4-9779c45a363c: The table of another similar manufacturer
opcoes.69bc9a8b-5d36-4bb9-847c-cb58b4578f27: The official load chart of the exact model

4398a3a1-daf8-4947-be85-7ff560925799.pergunta: What does UDL mean on a load chart?
4398a3a1-daf8-4947-be85-7ff560925799.explicacao: UDL is load spread over the entire length, the most favorable way to load the truss.

opcoes.d3ab4170-2869-47a7-9241-645278e1a116: Uniformly distributed load along the truss

d087b15b-edd8-4351-bf2a-6c4354e56972.pergunta: Why is a central point load more critical than a UDL of the same total?
d087b15b-edd8-4351-bf2a-6c4354e56972.explicacao: Concentrating the load at the center maximizes the bending moment, requiring a specific chart column, usually with a lower value.

opcoes.f879da0e-3f27-4861-951f-e4f70828ea1d: Because it concentrates the bending moment at the center

4c3f0f2a-3c14-42bb-97bb-9f0557a0d1f1.pergunta: What happens to the capacity when the free span is doubled?
4c3f0f2a-3c14-42bb-97bb-9f0557a0d1f1.explicacao: The bending moment grows with the square of the span; that is why the allowable load plummets and intermediate supports are used.

opcoes.b6ab90ec-1b1c-44e2-ba25-d7f51338aeb0: It drops drastically, because the moment grows with the square of the span

1efbb144-c81f-4679-85d6-d77400603042.explicacao: A large deflection is a warning: reduce load, shorten the span or add a support, respecting the chart limit.

opcoes.9182633d-1c7e-4f53-b3ca-c19c13ba4bdc: That you can double the load
opcoes.16ea4db7-6d50-40bb-bf11-4b9619697b45: That you are at the limit and should reduce load or span

48e504af-89eb-4e4b-a7ea-b48aadd12a5e.pergunta: When is it mandatory to call a qualified engineer?
48e504af-89eb-4e4b-a7ea-b48aadd12a5e.explicacao: Atypical scenarios fall outside the load chart's validity and require a calculation signed by an engineer with ART/RRT.

opcoes.39075e70-7be0-40fd-9184-2ab1df65b7fe: In configurations outside the catalog: cantilevers, outdoor wind, combined loads

e91d6fa7-a52e-4730-858e-44a35e55d5ff.pergunta: What is the R-clip (clip) on a cone joint?
e91d6fa7-a52e-4730-858e-44a35e55d5ff.explicacao: The R-clip locks the pin; a missing one is a serious failure because vibration can release the joint.

opcoes.cedc9a1c-e8dd-4346-9ec2-09ccc63676cc: A safety element that locks the pin and stops the joint from coming loose

6a005015-9893-40dc-b0de-3f6d2eed3853.pergunta: Can trusses from different brands be mixed freely?
6a005015-9893-40dc-b0de-3f6d2eed3853.explicacao: Fitting together does not mean structural compatibility; use official adapters and the lowest capacity involved.

opcoes.124228a4-86ec-4f95-868a-67c6d5725517: No; use official adapters and respect the lowest load chart

979d7354-023e-45f6-bec5-5c2b7b694794.pergunta: Why assemble the truss on the ground before hoisting?
979d7354-023e-45f6-bec5-5c2b7b694794.explicacao: Ground assembly is safer (less height, NR-35) and allows a full check of the connections.

opcoes.4b7db4f6-92d0-4d04-a94b-39d44a8e8828: It reduces work at height and allows checking all joints safely

f8afe16f-f125-47cb-9491-9892eade84a8.pergunta: Which bolt to use on a flange connection?
f8afe16f-f125-47cb-9491-9892eade84a8.explicacao: Ordinary bolts do not have the required strength class; use the specified one and the manufacturer's torque.

opcoes.5ce3d3c1-8fb8-4553-b8c9-5ad653c9d517: The bolt of the class and gauge specified by the manufacturer, at the correct torque

bbb499a2-56dd-48cd-b1e6-3ddcd176109e.pergunta: What does WLL mean on a sling or shackle?
bbb499a2-56dd-48cd-b1e6-3ddcd176109e.explicacao: WLL is the safe working load, already obtained by dividing the breaking load by a safety factor; never operate above it.

opcoes.9c8efbc1-bb02-40a8-bd7f-e80b27407876: Working Load Limit: safe working load, with a safety factor applied

4d14960b-db3f-4109-b97c-2b300d763c31.pergunta: What happens to the load in each leg when the sling angle is opened?
4d14960b-db3f-4109-b97c-2b300d763c31.explicacao: Wider angles raise the force in each leg of the sling, potentially exceeding the WLL if ignored.

a1e992a5-0bcf-4e91-bd7d-2f082059a3d0.pergunta: Redundancy (safety cable) over the public is:
a1e992a5-0bcf-4e91-bd7d-2f082059a3d0.explicacao: In rigging over people redundancy is required: if the primary path fails, the secondary holds the load.

opcoes.265f62ce-14e4-4235-a5b5-2bd6d9d72fc0: Mandatory — a second load path in case the primary fails

10e0a561-2891-491c-a730-49cd62b47900.pergunta: Which NR governs work at height during assembly?
10e0a561-2891-491c-a730-49cd62b47900.explicacao: NR-35 covers work at height: risk analysis, permit, PPE, anchorage and training.

c7308ede-4329-4148-a52e-fba98a1dad76.pergunta: Which shackle is acceptable in entertainment rigging?
c7308ede-4329-4148-a52e-fba98a1dad76.explicacao: Use rigging shackles marked with WLL (Crosby type/equivalent), with the pin fully tightened.

opcoes.87f02295-5460-4684-ab64-1bb0bd58ccce: A certified rigging shackle with marked WLL and a tightened pin
opcoes.1b318813-843b-448b-8b4c-f47be318913d: A rusty shackle if it is large

54943021-7476-408d-8512-1f348b24bc0a.pergunta: What distinguishes ground support from a flown structure?
54943021-7476-408d-8512-1f348b24bc0a.explicacao: Ground support is independent of the ceiling, but requires towers and stability; flown depends on the suspension point's capacity.

opcoes.817535d6-dbaf-40d1-8a7d-f696c4d1227e: Ground support does not need a motor
opcoes.2b1dbfc7-7509-45b6-bd99-24bf17eff3d6: Ground support rests on the floor via towers; flown hangs from ceiling points

dc8789a5-f3a1-4123-b7db-f010c9c4e312.pergunta: Why are screens and banners critical on an outdoor structure?
dc8789a5-f3a1-4123-b7db-f010c9c4e312.explicacao: The sail area amplifies the wind force, requiring more ballast/guy wires and influencing the whole calculation.

opcoes.cfb0a0ff-9f2f-4a47-a25c-9af7eb155cbf: They greatly increase the sail area and the wind force

dd25fe06-72f6-4c4e-a794-a7ff6ae222d6.pergunta: Who defines the amount of ballast and the guy-wire scheme?
dd25fe06-72f6-4c4e-a794-a7ff6ae222d6.explicacao: Ballast and guying depend on a calculation (sail area, design wind) done by a qualified engineer.

0cfe724d-1880-4228-9e34-7f9aeb2c3d42.pergunta: Which instrument triggers the wind management plan?
0cfe724d-1880-4228-9e34-7f9aeb2c3d42.explicacao: The anemometer measures wind speed and triggers the decisions to stop and lower the structure.

eca33ae5-d2d9-418e-9769-e35ae0ae692c.pergunta: What is the function of the RCD on the panel?
eca33ae5-d2d9-418e-9769-e35ae0ae692c.explicacao: The RCD detects leakage current (typically 30 mA for people) and trips quickly; it does not replace the breaker or the ground.

opcoes.42db3d6a-1d84-491a-93c7-75bb66c89a16: To protect people against shock from leakage current

bf5c206c-4f8f-4319-9b09-fdb3c903a670.explicacao: Distributing equal loads across the phases avoids overloading one and reduces the current in the neutral.

opcoes.adc431bc-87c8-4eae-9c77-65b8852a53ad: Distributing the loads evenly across L1/L2/L3

cd666446-049d-4d2b-8b51-aac9e898187f.pergunta: What happens if the cable is too thin for the current and distance?
cd666446-049d-4d2b-8b51-aac9e898187f.explicacao: Undersizing the gauge causes heating and voltage drop; on long runs the cross-section must be increased.

opcoes.67795005-e780-44e6-b1ab-be35e42088fc: It overheats and there is excessive voltage drop, making equipment fail

38ff9596-0213-497c-b9ac-d2153e660956.pergunta: What is the protective ground (PE) for?
38ff9596-0213-497c-b9ac-d2153e660956.explicacao: The ground drains faults and references the system, allowing the RCD/breaker to act and preventing shock.

opcoes.1dfcf98d-85b3-4111-be59-107dd15b6df6: To carry fault currents to earth and allow the protection to act, preventing energized enclosures

910d2692-3c29-4d67-a5bd-7d960cd39391.pergunta: Which standard governs safety in electrical installations at work?
910d2692-3c29-4d67-a5bd-7d960cd39391.explicacao: NR-10 covers electrical safety: authorized professionals, design, PPE and procedures.

2635a7c3-b844-4d32-aba3-471e7d3d2299.pergunta: Why not use a microphone cable for DMX on a long link?
2635a7c3-b844-4d32-aba3-471e7d3d2299.explicacao: DMX needs a data cable of ~120 ohm; a microphone cable has a different impedance and generates errors on long links.

opcoes.cc8a5509-ed87-4256-ab3e-f975bd66c68b: It does not fit the connector
opcoes.6ff327a1-69d7-4326-a5d0-ae9c4a2fc3bd: The wrong impedance (~120 ohm required) causes errors and flickering

aba3d19a-aaee-4405-8e47-1ef3c836cbb9.pergunta: Which connector is locking and suitable for loudspeaker power?
aba3d19a-aaee-4405-8e47-1ef3c836cbb9.explicacao: speakON is locking, with a gauge for power, avoiding exposing energized contacts.

ff4df3b9-b643-4d3c-b6b5-d9e177d6fe53.pergunta: For video at 40 m, what is the best option?
ff4df3b9-b643-4d3c-b6b5-d9e177d6fe53.explicacao: Passive HDMI is short; SDI or fiber cover long distances with quality.

4e73f97f-ad37-47fe-942e-6da4d561af69.pergunta: What is the practical limit of a run of copper Ethernet cable?
4e73f97f-ad37-47fe-942e-6da4d561af69.explicacao: Copper Ethernet has ~100 m per run; longer distances call for optical fiber.

1d5d2367-8ae8-409f-8f47-e82bf4e268d2.pergunta: How many channels does a DMX512 universe have?
1d5d2367-8ae8-409f-8f47-e82bf4e268d2.explicacao: A DMX512 universe (ANSI E1.11) has 512 channels, on a serial link with termination.

2db86d09-a1f9-45f0-9b0b-dd31ba527098.pergunta: What does RDM (E1.20) add to DMX?
2db86d09-a1f9-45f0-9b0b-dd31ba527098.explicacao: RDM runs on the same cable and allows configuring and diagnosing devices remotely.

opcoes.af638400-cc56-42ed-951b-0d79813947e8: Bidirectional communication: remote discovery, addressing and diagnosis

0c0e0611-e8d7-4f4f-adc9-9aabd34e0a77.pergunta: In the code IP65, what does the '6' and the '5' mean?
0c0e0611-e8d7-4f4f-adc9-9aabd34e0a77.explicacao: In IP65: first digit 6 = fully protected against dust; second 5 = protected against water jets.

opcoes.b0ae37ad-e72a-4e7c-8639-1abbb3345257: Protection against dust (6) and against water jets (5)

4f634346-bfec-46d7-81cd-eb49fb906050.pergunta: What is the IK rating for?
4f634346-bfec-46d7-81cd-eb49fb906050.explicacao: IK classifies resistance to impacts; it complements IP, which deals with dust and water.

opcoes.94e6ffb6-9f72-4efc-b435-7d690a86bfd9: Indicating the enclosure's resistance to mechanical impacts

f2cae12b-cd4b-49f1-9881-97d8e49f103f.pergunta: Which plug standard is robust and typical for event power?
f2cae12b-cd4b-49f1-9881-97d8e49f103f.explicacao: IEC 60309/CEE is the industrial standard with an IP rating and locking, ideal for event power.

6f2613c5-4d90-4b89-ad99-7e0f4ab94880.pergunta: Which situation NEVER has an acceptable improvised version?
6f2613c5-4d90-4b89-ad99-7e0f4ab94880.explicacao: Lifting over people requires a certified hoist, WLL and redundancy; improvisation here is prohibited.

457c1ec4-6716-4c73-85bd-85863ada38fd.pergunta: Why are the RCD and grounding not optional in the event's power?
457c1ec4-6716-4c73-85bd-85863ada38fd.explicacao: Without a ground and an RCD, faults are not detected and enclosures can become energized, causing shock and fire.
